base_case: Bull (melt-up intact but exhausting) — 7th consecutive ATH close, but the smallest daily gain of the streak; 30–60 day horizon still favors a grind to Wave 1 trim trigger at 7,696 (80.8% first per model) before a credit-driven mean-reversion becomes the dominant risk.
trend_regime: Golden Cross intact: 50-day above 200-day, price above both. +10.6% premium to 50-day SMA and +15.1% premium to 200-day SMA — both at cycle highs (extended).
valuation_bucket: 20–22x Forward P/E (21.75x) — Cautious posture; Wave 1 trim trigger (22x / 7,696) only 86 pts (1.13%) away. ERP ~+0.097% near the fundamental ceiling.

signals.bollinger_position: Upper band ride — pressed against the upper rail for 14 consecutive sessions
signals.vix_regime: Neutral (below 50-day MA of ~20; no elevated-risk flag)

what_changed_today: 7th consecutive ATH close at 7,609.78 (+9.82 / +0.13%) — smallest daily gain of the streak, a 35% shortfall vs the 15.1-pt streak average; momentum visibly decelerating.
RSI-14 closed at ~70 — the first ATH close below the overbought threshold this entire cycle, a fresh divergence crack.
Junk bond spread spiked sharply from ~1.33% to ~1.45% — now the widest since the April tariff shock; credit stress worsening while equities print new highs.
F&G fell to 57 from 59 (4th consecutive decline, down from 61 on May 26) even as SPX gained 90 pts over the window.
Wave 1 trim trigger proximity tightened from 96 pts to 86 pts; Monte Carlo P(W1 first) edged up to 80.8% from 79.6%; median days to Wave 1 ~4 (June 8–9).
Added explicit hard-stop logic: failed-breakout partial trim if SPX closes below 7,520 without executing Wave 1.
narrative_summary: SPX printed its seventh consecutive all-time high at 7,609.78, but the +0.13% gain was the weakest of the streak and RSI closed below 70 on an ATH day for the first time this cycle — clear signs of exhaustion. The single most important development is the junk bond spread spiking to ~1.45%, its widest since the April tariff shock that preceded a ~17% drawdown, even as F&G falls for a 4th straight session and breadth/price-strength stay in Fear. Monte Carlo still strongly favors a melt-up to the 7,696 Wave 1 trim trigger (80.8% first, median ~4 sessions), so the disciplined stance is to HOLD with a live GTC trim at 7,696 and be actively prepared to execute on touch — not to add here. The credit/equity divergence is the non-price risk the GBM model cannot capture, and its eventual resolution typically favors VIX spiking to meet credit rather than credit compressing to meet complacency.
open_questions: Will the junk bond spread divergence resolve via a credit-driven equity correction within the historical 10–15 session window, or compress back toward equity complacency?
Does SPX gap to or through 7,696 at Wednesday's open, forcing immediate Wave 1 execution?
Can McClellan breadth's 2-day modest improvement (~1,060) broaden enough to validate the new highs, or does Stock Price Strength stay stuck in Fear?
If SPX churns 7,550–7,640 for several sessions without touching Wave 1, does the setup decay toward the 7,520 failed-breakout stop?

decision_matrix.valuation: Forward P/E 21.75x (cautious band); ERP ~+0.097% sub-0.10% — fundamental ceiling, trim bias
decision_matrix.technicals: Golden Cross intact but extended (+10.6% / +15.1% to SMAs); 14-session upper-band ride; RSI ~70 first ATH close below OB; MFI flat-declining divergence
decision_matrix.sentiment: F&G 57 Greed (4th decline); Put/Call ~0.60 complacency; VIX ~15 neutral; junk spreads ~1.45% Extreme Fear — credit screaming danger vs equity calm
decision_matrix.risk: Leverage moderate; VIX below 50-day MA so no elevated flag, but VIX-15 vs 1.45% credit spread is a contradictory, fragile setup

confirming_evidence: Monte Carlo strongly favors melt-up: P(Wave 1 7,696 first vs 38.2% Fib) 80.8% / 19.0%; P(Wave 1 within 60d) 87.2%; median ~4 sessions to June 8–9.
Golden Cross fully intact — 50d > 200d, price above both, trend regime bullish.
Wave 1 trigger only 86 pts (1.13%) away with the model assigning near-certain probability of reaching it within 60 days.
P(7,700 first vs 7,400) at 66.6% — above the 65% action threshold, supporting the trim-on-touch obligation.
McClellan breadth posted a 2nd day of modest improvement (~1,060), a small offset to the breadth-Fear reading.

conflicting_evidence.bullish_read: Equities at the 7th consecutive ATH with VIX pinned at a cycle-low ~15 — no equity-vol stress visible.
conflicting_evidence.bearish_read: Junk bond spread spiked to ~1.45%, the widest since the April tariff shock that preceded a ~17% SPX drop; credit market pricing corporate stress while stocks ignore it.
conflicting_evidence.framework_rule: Layer 3C — spreads widening while equity holds is a credit-market warning and trim bias; resolution historically favors VIX spiking to meet credit.

conflicting_evidence.bullish_read: Price made a new all-time high at 7,620.90 — uptrend structure unbroken.
conflicting_evidence.bearish_read: RSI-14 closed at ~70, below overbought for the first time on an ATH day this cycle, and MFI is flat-to-declining — classic bearish momentum/volume divergence at the highs.
conflicting_evidence.framework_rule: Layer 2C — bearish divergence (momentum/MFI lower high vs price higher high) is trim confirmation.

conflicting_evidence.bullish_read: F&G still in Greed (57) with Market Momentum at Extreme Greed — risk appetite present.
conflicting_evidence.bearish_read: F&G fell 4 straight sessions (61→57) while SPX gained 90 pts; Put/Call flatlined at ~0.60 complacency; Stock Price Strength stuck in Fear 8 sessions — the crowd less excited at higher prices.
conflicting_evidence.framework_rule: Layer 3A — falling F&G into new price highs is a bearish sentiment divergence and trim-positioning signal.

conflicting_evidence.bullish_read: Earnings growth and Golden Cross support continued institutional dip-buying.
conflicting_evidence.bearish_read: ERP ~+0.097% — sub-0.10% for an 8th session, effectively negative on a real-return basis; the fundamental ceiling argues against adding aggressively.
conflicting_evidence.framework_rule: Layer 1A — ERP below 1.0%/near zero is a fundamental ceiling signal: trim bias, do not add aggressively.

conflicting_evidence.bullish_read: GBM model assigns 80.8% to Wave 1 first — strong melt-up edge above threshold.
conflicting_evidence.bearish_read: The GBM cannot incorporate the junk-spread spike and 4-session F&G decline; these non-price signals argue the rally is a last gasp.
conflicting_evidence.framework_rule: Layer 4 / Risk Rule 5 — Monte Carlo edge is necessary but not sufficient; honor non-price divergences and do not panic-buy.

primary_tension: A strong Monte Carlo melt-up edge (80.8% to Wave 1 first, trigger 7,696 just 86 pts away, median ~4 sessions) versus a sharply worsening credit-market warning — junk spreads at ~1.45%, their widest since the April shock that preceded a ~17% drop — compounded by a stack of cycle-extreme exhaustion and divergence signals (RSI first ATH close below 70, MFI divergence, F&G falling 4 sessions, ERP near zero, VIX-15 complacency). The disciplined resolution is to hold and stand ready to execute Trim Wave 1 on touch, not to add.
